title: MsgDecodeStart carries unbounded numeric fields with no validation schema — malicious worker messages can smuggle arbitrary integers
description: Fields like targetWidth, targetHeight, budgetMs, and downsample arrive over the postMessage channel from a worker and are typed as plain numbers or union literals. The TypeScript types enforce nothing at runtime: a compromised or malicious worker (or a fuzzer replaying messages) can send targetWidth=-1 or targetHeight=2^53. Consumers that use these values to allocate ArrayBuffers (e.g. pixels = new Uint8Array(width * height * 4)) can trigger OOM or integer-overflow allocations. No runtime guard or Zod/schema parse is present in this package to reject out-of-range values before they propagate downstream.

title: MsgDecodeError.code and MsgEncodeError.code are untyped strings — attacker-controlled error codes can bypass downstream switch/if dispatch
description: Both MsgDecodeError.code and MsgEncodeError.code (lines 139 and 278) are typed as plain `string`, not as the JxlErrorCode union. A compromised worker can send any arbitrary string as the error code. Downstream consumers that switch on these values for control flow (e.g. `if (msg.code === 'Cancelled') return`) will silently fall through to an unexpected branch. Constraining the type to JxlErrorCode is a trivial fix and prevents class of confused-deputy logic errors where error handling depends on exact code matching.

title: MsgEncodeError.code is also an untyped string (same issue as MsgDecodeError)
description: MsgEncodeError uses `code: string` instead of the JxlErrorCode union, mirroring the decode-side gap. Both error message types should reference the same controlled vocabulary to prevent dispatch confusion.

title: frameIndexing accepts an arbitrary string with only a JSDoc regex hint — no runtime enforcement
description: The EncodeOptions.frameIndexing field documents the constraint `^(0*|1[01]*)$` in a JSDoc comment but the TypeScript type is plain `string`. This means any string (including crafted inputs that could influence WASM bridge behavior in unexpected ways) passes the type system. If frameIndexing is forwarded to the WASM C++ bridge without sanitization, a value like a very long string of '1's could trigger unexpected behavior. A Zod schema or explicit guard at the session entry point should enforce the documented pattern.

title: jpegReconstruction.colorSpace is an unvalidated free-form string passed as a libjxl dec-hint
description: The colorSpace field in jpegReconstruction is typed as `string` and is documented as mapping to cjxl -x dec-hints (e.g., 'sRGB', 'RGB_D65_SRG_Per_SRG'). If this string is forwarded directly into the WASM bridge's hint processing without sanitization, an attacker-supplied value containing special characters or unusually long strings could potentially trigger unexpected C++ parsing behavior in libjxl. The risk is constrained to the WASM sandbox but worth validating against a whitelist at the session/facade boundary.
code_excerpt:
/** color_space from -x dec-hints (e.g. "sRGB", "RGB_D65_SRG_Per_SRG", shorthands). For raw input color or recon override. */
    colorSpace?: string;

title: No runtime message-validation schema (Zod/etc.) on the worker postMessage boundary
description: All worker↔main communication types are defined as TypeScript interfaces, which provide zero runtime protection. In a browser environment, message events can originate from any frame or worker with access to the port; in Node.js, any code can post to the worker. A Zod (or equivalent) schema for each discriminated union member would let the message router reject malformed or unexpected payloads before they reach decode/encode state machines, preventing logic bugs that arise from unanticipated field values. This is a standard defense-in-depth measure for postMessage APIs.

title: No evidence of automated dependency vulnerability scanning in CI for the jxl-core package
description: QUESTION: Is there a `npm audit` or `audit-ci` step in the CI pipeline for the jxl-core package (or the monorepo root)? This package re-exports all public contracts and is a dependency of several other packages. Without automated vulnerability scanning, a compromised transitive dependency (e.g., a malicious TypeScript toolchain package) would not be detected before distribution.
